title: The Lie You Were Told
idea:
I've sat across from hundreds of manual testers in 1:1 mentoring calls. The opening line is almost always some version of: "I'm not really a coder, so I don't know if automation is for me."

Here's what I tell every single one of them: you've been doing automation thinking since your first week as a tester. When you write a test case — Step 1: Open browser. Step 2: Go to login page. Step 3: Enter username. Step 4: Click login — that is an algorithm. You wrote it in English instead of JavaScript, but the logic is identical.

Playwright doesn't ask you to think differently. It asks you to write the same thinking in a syntax a machine can execute. That's the entire gap. Not "learn to code from zero." Just "learn to translate what you already know."
why:
I've watched two kinds of testers in my career. One waits two years "preparing" to start automation — reading, researching, never opening a code editor. The other opens VS Code in week one, writes broken code, and fixes it. The second one is always the one who gets hired first. Every time.

The market doesn't reward readiness. It rewards reps. TAE roles in India are growing faster than the supply of people who'll actually sit down and type the first line.

build.tool: A notebook or notes app — no code today, on purpose.
steps: Write your most-tested manual test case from memory. Any test case — login, search, checkout — anything you've run 10+ times in your career.
Write it as numbered steps. Be specific: what you open, what you type, what you click, what you check at the end.
Save it. You'll need it again on Day 4 — and you'll be surprised how little it changes when it becomes code.

reflection:
"Before today, I thought test automation was [X].
Today, I noticed that my manual test case looks like [Y].
That makes me feel [Z] about starting this journey.
Tomorrow, I will [W]."

No minimum word count. Write what's true, even if it's three lines. I'd rather have one honest sentence than a hundred performative words.

further.text: Playwright Docs — Introduction (just the first two sections, nothing more today)
Search "Manual vs Automation Testing" on YouTube. Pick any 5-minute explainer.

stretch: Write three test cases from memory, across different features or apps. Look at all three side by side. Notice the pattern: open, navigate, act, assert. I've never met a test case that doesn't reduce to that shape. That shape is what you're about to learn to write in code.
teaser: Tomorrow I'll show you the tool that's quietly taking over from Selenium in teams I've worked with — and why your browser is about to become its best friend.

title: Meet Playwright
idea:
Playwright is a browser automation tool built by Microsoft. It controls real browsers — Chrome, Firefox, Safari — using code.

I've used Selenium for years. I switched most of my teams to Playwright for one reason: it stops fighting you. Selenium makes you write manual waits — "wait 3 seconds for this button to appear." Playwright watches the page and waits automatically. That one feature alone eliminates half the flaky-test bugs I used to debug at midnight before a release.

It speaks JavaScript. JavaScript runs every website you've ever tested. That's not a coincidence — it means the language and the target are the same, which makes debugging far more intuitive than reaching for a separate language to test a JS-driven app.
why:
Every TAE job description I review now lists Playwright first or second, ahead of Selenium. That's not a trend I'm predicting — that's what I'm seeing in postings right now. Learning it isn't optional anymore. It's the floor.

What I want you to notice today: Playwright's documentation reads like instructions for a human, not a manual for a compiler. That's deliberate. The tool was built with testers in mind, not just developers.

steps: Go to playwright.dev. Spend 15 minutes on the homepage and the "Why Playwright?" section only.
Answer in your notes: what three browsers does Playwright support?
In Playwright's own words, what does "auto-wait" mean?
What's one feature that surprised you?

reflection:
"Before today, I thought automation tools were [X].
Today, I noticed that Playwright [Y].
That makes me feel [Z] about using it.
Tomorrow, I will [W]."

further.text: Playwright Docs — "Why Playwright?"
Search "Playwright in 100 seconds" on YouTube. Fireship's version is fast and accurate.

stretch: Read the last 3 entries in Playwright's release notes: playwright.dev/docs/release-notes. What's being added? What does that tell you about where the tool — and the skill demand — is heading?
teaser: Tomorrow we set up your machine — three installs, fifteen minutes, and you'll run your first automated browser action before the day ends.

title: Your Machine, Ready
idea:
Three things, and you're ready to write real automation:
• Node.js — the engine that runs JavaScript on your machine
• npm — the package manager that installs JavaScript tools
• Playwright — installed via npm

I've onboarded over a hundred testers onto automation stacks. The single biggest predictor of who finishes the cohort isn't prior coding experience. It's whether they get through setup day without quitting. Today is that day. Get through it.
why: I've seen brilliant testers stall for months at "I'll set it up this weekend." The weekend never comes. The people I've watched succeed are the ones who treat setup as a 20-minute task, not a project. It is a 20-minute task. Today proves that to you.

build.tool: Terminal (Command Prompt / PowerShell on Windows, Terminal on Mac/Linux) + your laptop
steps: Install Node.js: nodejs.org → download LTS → install. Verify: node --version (expect something like v20.x.x)

Create your project folder:
  mkdir automate-with-intent
  cd automate-with-intent

Install Playwright:
  npm init playwright@latest
  Choose: JavaScript · tests folder · yes to GitHub Actions · yes to install browsers

Run the example test:
  npx playwright test
  Watch it run. Watch it pass.

Open the report:
  npx playwright show-report
  You just ran your first automated test. You didn't write it. But you ran it — and that's the fear most people never get past.

reflection:
"Before today, I thought setting up a test automation environment was [X]. Today, I got through setup and noticed [Y]. That makes me feel [Z]. Tomorrow, I will [W]."

If something broke — write about that too. The errors you hit during setup are some of the most useful debugging reps you'll get all district.

further.text: Playwright Installation Guide
VS Code — if you don't already use a code editor

stretch: Open the example test file Playwright generated. Read it line by line. Don't aim to understand everything — just notice: does any line echo a step from your Day 1 manual test case?
teaser: That manual test case from Day 1 — tomorrow it becomes real code, and you watch a browser execute it on its own for the first time.

title: Your First Script
idea:
Every Playwright test I've ever written — and I've written thousands — breaks down into three moves you already know from manual testing:

ARRANGE — set up the conditions (open browser, go to URL)
ACT     — do the thing (click, type, navigate)
ASSERT  — check the result (did the right thing happen?)

This is the AAA pattern. It's not a Playwright concept — it's a testing concept that Playwright happens to express cleanly. You already think in AAA. Today you write it that way.
why: I remember my own first automated script. It wasn't elegant. It barely worked. But watching the browser do exactly what I told it to do — that's the moment I stopped being "a manual tester curious about automation" and started being someone who builds automation. That shift happens today for you too.

steps: Create: tests/day4-login.spec.js
Type the code below — type it, don't paste it. Typing builds muscle memory that copy-paste skips entirely.

Run: npx playwright test day4-login.spec.js --headed

The --headed flag means you watch the browser do it live.

code: const { test, expect } = require('@playwright/test'); test('user can log in to Sauce Demo', async ({ page }) => { // ARRANGE await page.goto('https://www.saucedemo.com'); // ACT await page.fill('#user-name', 'standard_user'); await page.fill('#password', 'secret_sauce'); await page.click('#login-button'); // ASSERT await expect(page).toHaveURL( 'https://www.saucedemo.com/inventory.html' ); });
reflection:
"Before today, I thought writing automation code was [X].
Today, I wrote my first Playwright test and noticed [Y].
That makes me feel [Z]. Tomorrow, I will [W]."

Specific question for your reflection: which line of code maps to which step in your Day 1 manual test case?

further.text: Playwright — Writing Tests
Search "AAA pattern testing" — any short explainer

teaser: Your test passes today. Tomorrow I'm going to make you break it on purpose — because reading failure well is the actual job.

title: The Art of Reading Failure
idea:
A passing test tells you one thing. A failing test — if you can read it — tells you everything.

I can spot a junior automation engineer in seconds: they panic at a red screen and start randomly changing code. A senior engineer reads the error message first, forms a hypothesis, then changes one thing. That's the entire difference, and it's learnable in a single afternoon. Today is that afternoon.
why: In your first TAE role, you'll spend more hours debugging failing tests than writing new ones — that's just the job. Teams don't hire people who can write tests. They hire people who can tell them, fast, whether a failing test means a real bug or a flaky script.

steps: BREAK 1 — Wrong URL: change the goto URL to an invalid path. Run. Read the error. Write down what it says. Fix it.
BREAK 2 — Wrong selector: change '#user-name' to '#username'. Run. Read the error. Fix it.
BREAK 3 — Wrong assertion: change the expected URL to something incorrect. Run. Read the error. Fix it.
For each: record the exact error, what Playwright expected vs. what it got, and how you diagnosed the fix.

reflection: "Before today, I thought test failure messages were [X]. Today, I noticed that error messages actually [Y]. That makes me feel [Z] about debugging in the future. Tomorrow, I will [W]."

further.text: Playwright — Debugging Tests
Search "how to read a stack trace" — any beginner explainer

stretch:
Run: npx playwright test day4-login.spec.js --debug

Step through the Inspector one action at a time. Notice how Playwright pauses and waits — this is the same mechanism behind the auto-wait feature from Day 2.
teaser: You've tested the user who does everything right. Tomorrow we test the ones who don't — because that's where the real bugs live.

title: Testing the Unhappy Path
idea:
Every test I review in a code audit falls into one of two buckets: happy-path-only, or genuinely useful. The difference is whether the engineer tested what breaks, not just what works.

Sauce Demo gives you three login failures to practice on: wrong password, locked-out user, empty fields. Each produces a different error message. Each deserves its own test.
why: In interviews, I ask candidates "what would you test here?" Juniors describe the happy path. The ones I hire describe what they expect to break. Your manual testing instinct — the part of you that always asks "but what if the user does X wrong" — is your biggest edge over someone who only knows how to code. Don't let automation make you forget that instinct. Encode it.

steps: Create: tests/day6-login-negative.spec.js
Write all three negative tests, using test.beforeEach() to avoid repeating the same navigation step in every test.
Run: npx playwright test day6-login-negative.spec.js --headed

code: const { test, expect } = require('@playwright/test'); test.beforeEach(async ({ page }) => { await page.goto('https://www.saucedemo.com'); }); test('locked out user sees error message', async ({ page }) => { await page.fill('#user-name', 'locked_out_user'); await page.fill('#password', 'secret_sauce'); await page.click('#login-button'); await expect(page.locator('[data-test="error"]')) .toContainText('locked out'); }); test('wrong password shows error', async ({ page }) => { await page.fill('#user-name', 'standard_user'); await page.fill('#password', 'wrong_password'); await page.click('#login-button'); await expect(page.locator('[data-test="error"]')) .toBeVisible(); }); test('empty fields shows error', async ({ page }) => { await page.click('#login-button'); await expect(page.locator('[data-test="error"]')) .toContainText('Username is required'); });
reflection: "Before today, I thought negative test cases in automation were [X]. Today, I noticed that test.beforeEach() [Y]. That makes me feel [Z] about my instinct as a tester. Tomorrow, I will [W]."

further.text: Playwright — test.beforeEach() and hooks
Search "positive vs negative testing" — any QA blog

stretch: Log in manually as 'performance_glitch_user' (password: secret_sauce). Watch what happens. Then write an automated test that asserts the inventory page eventually loads, even slowly. Look up Playwright's timeout option for this.
teaser: Six days, six tests. Tomorrow we stop coding and ask the question that actually matters: what did you become this week?

title: Connecting the Dots
recap:
Look back at your six reflections this week. Then write on:

– Day 1 you wrote a manual test case. Day 4 you ran it as code. What changed? What stayed the same?

– What surprised you most this week — about the tool, the process, or yourself?

– What did you resist? Setup? Typing instead of pasting? Be honest.

– What does "I am a test automation engineer" mean to you now, versus seven days ago?

No minimum word count. Write what's true.
community: Share one screenshot this week — first passing test, first error message, your HTML report. It doesn't need to be impressive. It needs to be real. I've mentored enough cohorts to know: the people who post the messy screenshot are the ones who finish.
rest:
You installed tools, wrote real code, broke things on purpose, and fixed them. That's a real week of engineering work.

Rest is allowed. It's also part of the job — burnt-out testers write worse tests.
teaser: District 2 starts Monday. Just enough JavaScript to make Playwright do anything you want — taught the way I'd teach it in a 1:1, not the way a bootcamp would.

title: Variables Are Just Labelled Boxes
why: I review a lot of test code from junior automation engineers. The single most common smell I see is the same string typed six different times across one file. Then the password changes, and five of the six get updated. The sixth test starts failing for a reason nobody can find in ten minutes of looking. Variables aren't a JavaScript concept you learn for an exam. They're how you stop creating bugs in your own test suite.

steps: Refactor day4-login.spec.js using variables. Open the file and rewrite it using const for username, password, and baseUrl.
Use the variables everywhere you previously hardcoded the string — in page.fill() calls and in your assertion.
Run it. Confirm it still passes. Nothing about the test's behaviour changed — only how it's written. That's the whole point: same outcome, fewer places a typo can hide.

code: const { test, expect } = require('@playwright/test'); test('user can log in to Sauce Demo', async ({ page }) => { const username = 'standard_user'; const password = 'secret_sauce'; const baseUrl = 'https://www.saucedemo.com'; await page.goto(baseUrl); await page.fill('#user-name', username); await page.fill('#password', password); await page.click('#login-button'); await expect(page).toHaveURL(baseUrl + '/inventory.html'); });
reflection:
"Before today, I thought variables in code were [X].
Today, I noticed that refactoring with const made my test [Y].
That makes me feel [Z] about reading other people's test code.
Tomorrow, I will [W]."

further.text: MDN — "Storing the information you need — Variables." Just the first half. Stop once you understand const vs let.

stretch:
baseUrl + '/inventory.html' is called string concatenation — joining two strings together. There's a cleaner modern way to do this called a template literal: backtick-wrapped ${baseUrl}/inventory.html

Try rewriting your assertion using backticks and ${} instead of +. Which reads easier to you? There's no wrong answer — but you'll see backtick syntax constantly in other people's code, so recognising it matters even if you don't use it yet.
teaser: You've been writing the same three lines of login code in every test file. Tomorrow I show you how to write it exactly once.

title: Functions: Write It Once, Use It Everywhere
why: This is the first moment your code starts looking like a real test suite instead of a pile of scripts. I've inherited test suites with the same 12 lines copy-pasted across 40 files. The day someone changes a CSS selector, all 40 files break, and whoever's on call that week spends their evening fixing the same bug 40 times. A function fixes the bug once.

steps: Create a new file: tests/day9-login-function.spec.js
Write an async function called login(page, username, password) that does the navigate + fill + fill + click steps.
Write two test() blocks that each call login() with different credentials — one with standard_user, one with locked_out_user.
Notice: two tests, one login function, zero duplication. Run both and confirm they pass.

code: const { test, expect } = require('@playwright/test'); async function login(page, username, password) { await page.goto('https://www.saucedemo.com'); await page.fill('#user-name', username); await page.fill('#password', password); await page.click('#login-button'); } test('standard user logs in successfully', async ({ page }) => { await login(page, 'standard_user', 'secret_sauce'); await expect(page).toHaveURL( 'https://www.saucedemo.com/inventory.html' ); }); test('locked out user sees error', async ({ page }) => { await login(page, 'locked_out_user', 'secret_sauce'); await expect(page.locator('[data-test="error"]')) .toContainText('locked out'); });
reflection:
"Before today, I thought reusing code meant [X].
Today, I noticed that writing the login function [Y].
That makes me feel [Z] about the tests I wrote in District 1.
Tomorrow, I will [W]."

further.text: MDN — "Functions — reusable blocks of code"

stretch: Add a third parameter to login() called expectedUrl, and move the assertion inside the function itself. Now login() both logs in AND confirms it worked. What's the tradeoff of putting an assertion inside a reusable function instead of in the test? Think about it before you Google it.
teaser: You're about to test the same login function against six different users without writing six different tests by hand.

title: If This, Then That
idea:
Every manual test plan has branches. "If the user is locked out, expect an error. If not, expect the inventory page." You say this in plain English constantly. In code, this is an if/else statement.

Read it exactly like English: "if the username equals locked out user, do this. Otherwise, do that." === means "is exactly equal to" — note the three equals signs, not one. One equals sign (=) assigns a value into a box. Three equals signs (===) compares two values. Mixing these up is the single most common beginner JS bug, and I still see senior engineers do it occasionally under deadline pressure.
why: This is the moment your reflection prompt's own Mad Lib frame — "Before today I thought X, today I noticed Y" — and an if/else statement are doing the same job: branching based on a condition. You already think in conditionals. Code conditionals just need precise syntax where English allows ambiguity.

steps: Create: tests/day10-conditional-login.spec.js
Write one function loginAndCheck(page, username) that logs in, then uses if/else to assert the correct outcome depending on whether username is locked_out_user or not.
Call this function from two separate test() blocks — one with 'standard_user', one with 'locked_out_user'.
Run both tests. Confirm both pass — using the same function, branching to two different outcomes.

code: async function loginAndCheck(page, username) { await page.goto('https://www.saucedemo.com'); await page.fill('#user-name', username); await page.fill('#password', 'secret_sauce'); await page.click('#login-button'); if (username === 'locked_out_user') { await expect(page.locator('[data-test="error"]')).toBeVisible(); } else { await expect(page).toHaveURL('https://www.saucedemo.com/inventory.html'); } }
reflection:
"Before today, I thought conditional logic in code was [X].
Today, I noticed that === behaves differently from = because [Y].
That makes me feel [Z]. Tomorrow, I will [W]."

further.text: MDN — "Making decisions in your code — conditionals"

stretch: Sauce Demo also has 'problem_user' and 'performance_glitch_user'. Extend your if/else into an if/else if/else chain that handles all four usernames with the correct expected outcome for each. You'll need to actually log in manually as problem_user first to see what breaks — I won't tell you. Go find out.
teaser: Four if/else branches for four users is already getting messy. Tomorrow I show you the pattern that replaces all four branches with one loop — and it's the single biggest line-count reducer you'll learn in this entire cohort.

title: Loops: Stop Writing the Same Test Six Times
why: The first time I saw a junior engineer write the same assertion eight times with only the product name changed, I sat with them for twenty minutes and we turned it into a five-line loop. They told me afterward it was the first time automation "clicked" for them — because the repetition is exactly what you'd avoid doing manually too. A loop is just automating your own impatience with repetition.

steps: Create: tests/day11-product-loop.spec.js
Log in, then use page.locator('.inventory_item_name').allTextContents() to extract all product names into an array.
Loop over the array with for (const name of productNames) and console.log() each one.
Run with --headed and watch the terminal output. You just extracted real data from the page and looped over it.

code: const { test, expect } = require('@playwright/test'); test('all products show an add to cart button', async ({ page }) => { await page.goto('https://www.saucedemo.com'); await page.fill('#user-name', 'standard_user'); await page.fill('#password', 'secret_sauce'); await page.click('#login-button'); const productNames = await page .locator('.inventory_item_name') .allTextContents(); for (const name of productNames) { console.log('Checking product:', name); } expect(productNames.length).toBeGreaterThan(0); });
reflection:
"Before today, I thought looping through page elements was [X].
Today, I noticed that allTextContents() gave me [Y].
That makes me feel [Z] about testing pages with lots of repeated elements.
Tomorrow, I will [W]."

further.text: MDN — "Loops and iteration"

stretch: Inside your loop, assert that each product name is not an empty string: expect(name.length).toBeGreaterThan(0). Now you're not just printing data — you're asserting against every single product automatically, no matter how many Sauce Demo adds in the future. That's the real power of this pattern: it scales without you touching the test again.
teaser: You've been finding elements with simple selectors like #user-name. Tomorrow I show you how Playwright actually finds elements on a messy real-world page — and why some selectors break the moment a developer changes one CSS class.

title: Selectors: How Playwright Finds Things
idea:
Every page.fill() and page.click() you've written needs to know WHERE on the page to act. That's what a selector is — an address that tells Playwright "click this exact element, not any other one."

You've used CSS selectors like '#user-name' without me naming them. Today you learn the selector types you'll actually use, ranked by how much I trust them:

1. data-test attributes — [data-test="error"]. Most reliable. Built specifically for testing.
2. IDs — #user-name. Reliable if unique on the page.
3. Text content — page.getByText('Add to cart'). Readable, but breaks if copy changes.
4. CSS classes — .inventory_item_name. Fragile. Use as a last resort.
why: I've debugged more flaky test suites caused by bad selectors than by any other single issue. A developer renames a CSS class for a redesign, and forty tests fail overnight — not because the feature broke, but because the test was anchored to the wrong thing. The selector you choose is a contract with the developer. data-test attributes are the strongest contract you can ask for.

steps: Open saucedemo.com in Chrome. Right-click the "Add to cart" button on any product → Inspect. Find its data-test attribute.
Create: tests/day12-selectors.spec.js
Write a test that logs in, then uses a data-test selector to click "Add to cart" on the first product (hint: data-test="add-to-cart-sauce-labs-backpack").
Assert the cart badge shows "1" using selector .shopping_cart_badge. Run with --headed and watch the cart badge update.

reflection: "Before today, I thought all selectors were roughly equally reliable. Today, I noticed that data-test attributes [Y]. That makes me feel [Z] about the selectors I used back in District 1. Tomorrow, I will [W]."

further.text: Playwright — Locators (the official guide to every selector strategy Playwright supports)

stretch: Go back through every test file you've written since Day 4. For each selector, classify it: data-test, ID, text, or class. Pick the weakest one in your whole codebase and rewrite it using a stronger strategy. This is exactly the kind of pass I'd ask a junior engineer to do in a code review.
teaser: You've been testing one thing at a time. Tomorrow you connect everything from this week — variables, functions, conditionals, loops, selectors — into a single test that mimics a real shopping journey from login to checkout.

title: The Full Journey
idea:
No new concept today. Today you combine everything from this district into one realistic test: a user logs in, adds a product to their cart, and proceeds to checkout — the actual shape of a real Sauce Demo user journey, and the actual shape of most automation tests you'll write in your first TAE job.

This is what I look for in a portfolio project: not six isolated snippets, but one coherent flow that proves you can chain steps together the way a real user — and a real test plan — would.
why: Interviewers don't ask "can you click a button." They ask "walk me through a test you'd write for a checkout flow." Today you build the answer to that exact question, and you build it using only things you've learned and typed yourself this week. Nothing here is new syntax — it's assembly.

steps: Create: tests/day13-full-journey.spec.js
Using a login() function (from Day 9), write one test that logs in as standard_user.
Add the Sauce Labs Backpack to the cart using a data-test selector (from Day 12). Assert the cart badge shows "1."
Click the cart icon, navigate to the cart page, click "Checkout."
Fill in first name, last name, postal code. Click "Continue." Assert the page shows "Checkout: Overview."
You'll need to inspect each new page's elements in DevTools as you go — I'm not handing you every selector this time. Finding them yourself is the actual skill.

reflection: "Before today, I thought chaining multiple steps into one test would be [X]. Today, I noticed that building the full checkout flow [Y]. That makes me feel [Z] about writing tests for real features at work. Tomorrow, I will [W]."

further.text: Playwright — Actions (covers fill, click, check, select, and the other interactions you used today)

stretch: Refactor the checkout form steps into their own function, the same way you did with login() on Day 9. Now you have two reusable functions: login() and checkout(). This is the exact shape of Page Object Model thinking — you'll formalise this pattern properly in District 6, but you've already started doing it instinctively.
teaser: Six days of new syntax. Tomorrow, no code — just the question that matters more than any keyword: do you actually think differently about test code now than you did a week ago?

title: Connecting the Dots
recap:
Look back at your six reflections this week. Then write on:

– You refactored Day 4's test with variables on Day 8, and built a full checkout journey by Day 13. Reread both files side by side if you still have them. What does the difference tell you?

– Which concept this week clicked fastest — variables, functions, conditionals, loops, or selectors? Which one resisted you the longest?

– On Day 12 you ranked selectors by reliability. Has that changed how you'll write tests going forward, even outside this cohort?

– A recruiter asks you: "what's the difference between a manual test case and a Playwright test?" After this week, what's your honest answer?

No minimum word count. Write what's true.
community: Post your Day 13 full-journey test in the community — the whole file, not just a screenshot. This is the first piece of code in the cohort worth someone else reading closely. I'll be looking for selector choices specifically — and so should you, on each other's posts.
rest:
This was a heavier week than District 1. You went from "I can run a test someone else wrote" to "I can build a multi-step journey from scratch." That's not a small jump.

Let it land before you move on.
teaser: District 3 starts Monday. You know how to write a test. Now I teach you how to organise dozens of them — and the first habit I drill into every mentee before their test suite gets out of hand.

title: A Passing Test Is Not the Same as a Working Feature
idea:
I once watched a test suite go fully green the same week a payment bug shipped to production. Every test passed. The feature was still broken. How?

The tests only checked the happy path with one specific input. Nobody had asked: what if the amount is zero? What if it's negative? What if two requests fire at once? The tests weren't wrong. They were just answering a narrower question than anyone realized.

A passing test tells you: "the specific thing I checked, behaved the way I expected, under the specific conditions I set up." That's it. It says nothing about the things you didn't think to check. The gap between "tests pass" and "feature works" is exactly the size of your test design.
why: This is the conceptual shift that takes someone from "writes automation scripts" to "designs test coverage." Companies don't pay TAEs to type page.click() correctly — they pay them to decide what 50 lines of test code should actually verify out of the hundreds of things that could go wrong. That decision is the actual skill. Everything else is execution.

steps: Pick ONE feature on Sauce Demo: the login form. Spend 20 minutes manually trying to break it — not automating anything, just clicking around like a curious, slightly mischievous user.

Try: special characters in the username field, extremely long input, pasting whitespace-only text, rapid double-clicking login, submitting with JavaScript disabled (DevTools → Settings → Disable JavaScript, if you're comfortable).
Write down every distinct behavior you observe — not just bugs. "Nothing visibly happened" is also a finding worth recording.

reflection:
"Before today, I thought a passing test meant [X].
Today, I noticed that manually exploring the login form revealed [Y].
That makes me feel [Z] about the six tests I already automated for this exact form.
Tomorrow, I will [W]."

further.text: Search "exploratory testing vs scripted testing" — any solid QA blog comparing the two approaches.

stretch: Of everything you found today, pick the single most interesting behavior — bug or not. Write one sentence explaining why it matters, the way you'd explain it to a product manager who has 30 seconds to listen.
teaser: You found real gaps today with nothing but curiosity and your hands. Tomorrow I give you a framework so you stop relying on luck to find them.

title: The Boundary Value Framework
idea:
Most bugs don't live in the middle of normal behavior. They live at the edges — the boundaries. If a field accepts 1 to 10 items, the bug is rarely at 5. It's at 0, 1, 10, and 11.

This is called boundary value analysis, and it's the single highest-leverage test design technique I know. Instead of guessing randomly at what might break, you deliberately target:

– The minimum valid value
– One below the minimum (invalid)
– The maximum valid value
– One above the maximum (invalid)
– Empty / zero / null where applicable

Sauce Demo's checkout form requires a postal code. What's the minimum length that's accepted? Is there a maximum? What happens at exactly the boundary?
why: I ask this in nearly every TAE interview I run: "this field accepts quantities 1 through 99. What test cases do you write?" Candidates who say "I'd test a few numbers like 5, 20, 50" get a polite nod. Candidates who say "I'd test 0, 1, 99, 100, and negative numbers" get the next round. Boundary thinking is the fastest tell of someone who's actually tested software before, versus someone who's only ever written code.

steps: Create: tests/day16-boundary-quantity.spec.js
On the cart page, inspect the quantity field's constraints by examining it in DevTools first.

Write a test using a loop (from Day 11) that tries multiple boundary values for postal code on the checkout form: empty string, a single character, a very long string (50+ characters), and a normal value like "600001."
Log what happens for each. Note which ones the app silently accepts that probably shouldn't be accepted.

reflection:
"Before today, I thought edge cases were [X].
Today, I noticed that testing the postal code boundaries revealed [Y].
That makes me feel [Z] about how I'll design tests going forward.
Tomorrow, I will [W]."

further.text: Search "boundary value analysis testing examples" — any explainer with concrete before/after examples.

stretch: Apply boundary thinking to a field you haven't tested yet: the username field on login. What's the longest username Sauce Demo will accept before truncating or rejecting it? Find the boundary experimentally, then write one test that locks in that boundary as a known, documented behavior.
teaser: You've been hand-typing every test value. Tomorrow you separate your test data from your test logic — and your tests get easier to extend than you'd expect.

title: Separate Your Data from Your Logic
why: This pattern is how real test suites scale to hundreds of cases without hundreds of nearly-identical files. I've seen test suites with 200 test() blocks that were really the same 12 lines of logic repeated with different data. The moment you separate data from logic, adding a new test case becomes "add one line to an array" instead of "copy-paste 15 lines and hope you didn't introduce a typo."

steps: Create: tests/day17-data-driven-postal.spec.js
Rebuild yesterday's postal code test using the array + loop pattern. Each entry should generate its own named test().

When you run npx playwright test, you should see four separate test results in the report, each labeled clearly (empty, single character, fifty characters, normal value), even though you only wrote the test logic once.
Run it and check the HTML report. Confirm all four show up as distinct, individually named test results.

code: const postalCodes = [ { value: '', label: 'empty' }, { value: '1', label: 'single character' }, { value: '6'.repeat(50), label: 'fifty characters' }, { value: '600001', label: 'normal value' } ]; for (const code of postalCodes) { test(`postal code: ${code.label}`, async ({ page }) => { // navigate to checkout, fill postal code with code.value // assert the expected outcome for this case }); }
reflection:
"Before today, I thought writing many similar test cases meant [X].
Today, I noticed that the array + loop pattern [Y].
That makes me feel [Z] about extending test coverage in the future.
Tomorrow, I will [W]."

further.text: Playwright — Parameterize tests

stretch: Take your Day 6 negative login tests (locked out, wrong password, empty fields) and rebuild them using this same data-driven array + loop pattern. Compare line count before and after. I guarantee it's shorter — and easier to extend with a fifth username case later.
teaser: Every assertion you've written so far checks one specific thing. Tomorrow I show you how to choose assertions that actually catch bugs instead of just technically passing.

title: Weak Assertions Lie to You
why: I've audited test suites that were 90% weak assertions — toBeVisible() everywhere, almost no toContainText() or toHaveValue() checking actual content. Those suites gave teams false confidence. Tests were green, demos went fine, and real bugs slipped past because the tests were only checking that "something" rendered, not that the right thing rendered. Writing strong assertions is judgment, not syntax — and it's exactly what separates tests that catch bugs from tests that just look busy.

steps: Open your day6-login-negative.spec.js (or its Day 17 data-driven rebuild). Audit every assertion in the file.

For each one, ask: if the wrong thing happened here, would this assertion actually catch it? Rewrite any weak assertions (toBeVisible() alone) into strong ones (toContainText() with the specific expected message, or toHaveValue() with the exact expected value).
Create: tests/day18-strong-assertions.spec.js with at least three rewritten assertions, and a one-line comment above each explaining what weak version it replaced and why the new one is stronger.

reflection: "Before today, I thought all passing assertions were equally trustworthy. Today, I noticed that auditing my own tests for weak assertions revealed [Y]. That makes me feel [Z] about the tests I wrote in Districts 1 and 2. Tomorrow, I will [W]."

further.text: Playwright — Assertions (the full list of available matchers, worth skimming once)

stretch: Find one assertion anywhere in your codebase that checks a URL with toHaveURL() but nothing else. Add a second, content-level assertion right after it — something that confirms the page didn't just navigate, but actually loaded the right content. Two assertions, two different failure modes covered.
teaser: You've tested forms and buttons. Tomorrow you test something that doesn't behave the same way twice — and you'll meet your first real flaky test.

title: Flaky Tests and How to Stop Fearing Them
idea:
A flaky test passes sometimes and fails other times, with no change to the code being tested. It's not a bug in the app — it's a bug in the test. Today you meet one on purpose.

Sauce Demo's performance_glitch_user (from Day 6's Stretch) loads slowly and unpredictably. Write a test against it with a short, tight timeout, and watch it fail intermittently. That instability is exactly what "flaky" means.

The fix usually isn't "add more waits everywhere." It's understanding WHY the test is unreliable: is it racing the page load? Asserting before an animation finishes? Depending on test execution order? Each cause has a different fix.
why: Flaky tests are the single biggest reason teams stop trusting their automation suite. I've watched entire teams start ignoring failing tests — "oh that one's just flaky, ignore it" — until a real failure gets ignored along with the noise. A TAE who can diagnose and fix flakiness is worth more to a team than one who can only write new tests, because flakiness left unfixed eventually poisons the whole suite's credibility.

steps: Create: tests/day19-flaky-glitch-user.spec.js
Write a test logging in as performance_glitch_user with an artificially short timeout: page.goto(url, { timeout: 2000 })
Run it 5 times in a row: npx playwright test day19-flaky-glitch-user.spec.js --repeat-each=5

Document the pass/fail pattern. Then fix it properly — not by blindly increasing the timeout to something huge, but by using Playwright's auto-wait correctly: assert on the inventory page element appearing, rather than racing a fixed clock.

reflection:
"Before today, I thought flaky tests were [X].
Today, I noticed that running the same test 5 times revealed [Y].
That makes me feel [Z] about trusting a single test run as proof something works.
Tomorrow, I will [W]."

further.text: Playwright — Test retries and auto-waiting

stretch: Search your own codebase for any hardcoded page.waitForTimeout() calls — fixed-duration waits. Replace each one with a proper Playwright auto-wait (waiting for a specific element or condition instead of a fixed number of milliseconds). Explain in your reflection why this is more reliable, not just shorter.
teaser: You now know how to find good test cases, write strong assertions, and diagnose instability. Tomorrow you put it all together and design — not just write — a test plan for a feature you've never tested before.

title: Design Before You Code
idea:
No new technique today. Today you use everything from this district on a feature of Sauce Demo you haven't deliberately tested yet: the sort dropdown on the inventory page (Name A-Z, Z-A, Price Low-High, High-Low).

Before writing a single line of code, write a test PLAN first — on paper or in your notes. List every case you'd want covered: each sort option works correctly, the default sort on page load, sorting after adding items to cart, sorting with only one product visible (if you can simulate it), boundary thinking applied to a dropdown instead of a text field.

This is the actual job. Code comes second. Thinking comes first.
why: I've watched engineers open VS Code before they've decided what they're testing, and end up with three overlapping tests that all check the same thing while missing an obvious gap. Five minutes of planning on paper saves thirty minutes of rewriting code later. This is the habit that separates people who automate fast from people who automate well — and ideally, you want both, but if you can only have one starting out, choose well.

steps: Step 1 — Write your test plan for the sort dropdown. Minimum 5 distinct test cases, each with a one-line description of what it verifies and why it matters.

Step 2 — Implement at least 3 of those 5 cases as real Playwright tests in tests/day20-sort-dropdown.spec.js, using data-driven patterns (Day 17) and strong assertions (Day 18) where they fit naturally.
Step 3 — Run them. Confirm they pass against the real behavior of the dropdown.

reflection: "Before today, I thought planning tests before coding them was [X]. Today, I noticed that writing the plan first changed [Y] about how I approached the sort dropdown. That makes me feel [Z]. Tomorrow, I will [W]."

further.text: Search "test case design techniques" — any QA blog covering equivalence partitioning alongside boundary value analysis (you've now used both, even if you didn't know the second name).

stretch: Implement your remaining 2 test cases from the plan. Then look back at the full file: does it read like a coherent test strategy for the sort feature, or like a loose pile of individual checks? If it's the latter, what would you reorder or rename to make the intent clearer to someone reading it cold?
teaser: Three districts down. Tomorrow, no code — just an honest look at whether you're starting to think like the engineer you're becoming, not just type like one.

title: Connecting the Dots

Look back at your six reflections this week. Then write on:

– Day 15 you found bugs with nothing but curiosity. Day 20 you designed a structured test plan before writing code. What changed about how you approach a feature you've never seen?

– Boundary values, data-driven tests, strong assertions, flaky test diagnosis — which of these four do you reach for instinctively now, and which still feels like a checklist you have to consciously remember?

– What's the difference, in your own words, between "I can write a Playwright test" and "I can test a feature"?

No minimum word count. Write what's true.
community: Post your Day 20 test plan — the written plan, not just the code. I want to see how people are thinking before they automate, not just what they shipped. This is the most useful thing you can share with each other this week.
rest:
This district asked more of your judgment than your typing. That kind of thinking is genuinely tiring in a different way than syntax practice.

Let it settle.
teaser: District 4 starts Monday. You've been testing the same Sauce Demo journey by hand, one script at a time. I'm about to show you how real teams make automation run itself — without you sitting there watching it.

title: You've Been Automating Tests. Not a Test Suite.
idea:
Open your tests folder right now. Count the files. You probably have somewhere between 8 and 12 .spec.js files, named after the day you wrote them — day4-login, day9-login-function, day17-data-driven-postal. That naming made sense while you were learning. It makes zero sense to a teammate who joins next month and has no idea what "day17" means.

A test suite isn't just "all your test files in one folder." It's an organized collection where the structure itself tells you something — which tests cover which feature, which ones run fast versus slow, which ones are safe to skip when you're in a hurry. Today you stop organizing by "when I wrote it" and start organizing by "what it tests."
why: I've onboarded onto codebases with 300 test files named test1.spec.js through test300.spec.js. Finding the test that covers checkout took longer than just rewriting it from scratch. A test suite that only the original author can navigate isn't really automation — it's a liability with a green checkmark on it. The way you organize your tests is part of the deliverable, not an afterthought.

build.tool: VS Code, your existing tests folder
steps: Create three new folders inside tests/: auth/, inventory/, checkout/.

Go through every file you've written since Day 4 and move it into the folder matching what it actually tests — login and negative-login tests go in auth/, product-listing and sort-dropdown tests go in inventory/, the full-journey and data-driven-postal tests go in checkout/.
Rename each file to describe its content instead of its day: day4-login.spec.js becomes auth/login.spec.js.
Run npx playwright test after the move and confirm everything still passes — Playwright finds tests recursively, so this reorganization shouldn't break anything.

reflection:
"Before today, I thought my test files were [X].
Today, I noticed that reorganizing by feature instead of by day made my tests folder [Y].
That makes me feel [Z] about how I'll structure new tests from now on.
Tomorrow, I will [W]."

further.text: Playwright — Test configuration (covers how Playwright discovers and organizes test files)

stretch: Open playwright.config.js. Find the testDir setting. Read what it controls. Then look at the testMatch or testIgnore options in the docs — could you exclude a folder from a normal test run without deleting it? Why might that be useful for a folder of slow or experimental tests?
teaser: Your folders are organized now. Tomorrow you learn the command that lets you run just ONE of those folders — because nobody runs the entire suite every single time, and you shouldn't either.

title: Run What You Need, Not Everything
idea:
If you're only changing the checkout flow today, why wait for your auth tests to run too? Playwright lets you target exactly what you need:

npx playwright test tests/checkout — runs only the checkout folder
npx playwright test --grep "postal code" — runs only tests whose name contains "postal code"
npx playwright test tests/auth/login.spec.js — runs one specific file

This isn't a shortcut for lazy people. It's how every experienced engineer actually works day to day — full suite runs are for CI and final checks, not for the 50 times a day you're iterating on one feature.
why: I've watched junior engineers run the entire suite — sometimes 8 or 10 minutes — just to check if one assertion they changed now passes. That's 10 minutes of waiting, dozens of times a day, that should have been 10 seconds. Knowing how to scope a test run isn't a nice-to-have skill. It's the difference between a productive day and a day spent staring at a progress bar.

steps: Run each of these and note how many tests executed and how long each took: npx playwright test tests/auth, npx playwright test tests/inventory, npx playwright test tests/checkout
Also run: npx playwright test --grep "boundary" and npx playwright test --grep "locked out"

Add a one-line comment at the top of your three messiest or slowest test files explaining, in your own words, what that file covers — the kind of comment that saves a teammate from opening the file just to find out.

reflection: "Before today, I thought running my whole test suite every time was [X]. Today, I noticed that scoping test runs with --grep and folder paths made my workflow [Y]. That makes me feel [Z] about how I'll work day to day in a real job. Tomorrow, I will [W]."

further.text: Playwright — Command line (the full list of run options, including --grep, --headed, --debug, and more)

stretch: Open package.json. Add a "scripts" entry like: "test:auth": "playwright test tests/auth". Now you can run npm run test:auth instead of typing the full path every time. Add similar shortcuts for inventory and checkout. This is exactly what real teams do to make common commands fast to reach for.
teaser: You've been running tests on your own machine this whole time. Tomorrow your tests run somewhere you've never even logged into — and you don't have to be there when it happens.

title: Your First Test Run Without You
idea:
Every test you've run so far has run because you typed a command on your laptop. Today, your tests run on a machine owned by GitHub, triggered by you pushing code — not by you running anything.

This is GitHub Actions: a system that watches your repository and runs commands automatically when something happens, like a push. When you ran npm init playwright@latest back on Day 3, it already generated a basic GitHub Actions config for you — you just haven't used it yet. It lives at .github/workflows/playwright.yml.

Think of it like this: you've been the only person who could "press play" on your tests. Today you hire a robot that presses play automatically, every time code changes — and reports back whether things still work, whether you're awake to ask or not.
why: This is the single feature that turns "I wrote some automated tests" into "I built a safety net for the team." A test suite that only runs when someone remembers to run it manually gets skipped under deadline pressure — I've watched it happen constantly. A test suite that runs automatically on every push catches problems whether anyone remembers to check or not. This is what "CI" — continuous integration — actually means in practice, not just as a buzzword on your resume.

steps: If you haven't already, create a GitHub account and push your automate-with-intent project to a new repository (search "git push existing project to new github repo" if you need the exact commands).

Once pushed, open .github/workflows/playwright.yml in VS Code. Read through it line by line — you'll recognize on: push as "when does this run," and the steps as roughly the same setup you did manually on Day 3.
Push a small change (even just a comment) to trigger it. Go to your repository on GitHub.com, click the "Actions" tab, and watch your tests run on a machine you've never logged into.

reflection:
"Before today, I thought CI/CD was [X].
Today, I noticed that watching my tests run on GitHub's servers, without me typing any command, made me feel [Y].
That makes me feel [Z] about what 'automation' actually means.
Tomorrow, I will [W]."

further.text: GitHub Actions — Understanding GitHub Actions

stretch: In the Actions tab, click into your test run and find the HTML report artifact. Download it and open it locally — same report format you've seen all cohort, except this one was generated by a server, not your laptop. That's the proof: the exact same tests, running in a place you don't control, behaving the same way they do for you.
teaser: Right now your tests only run when you push code. Tomorrow I show you how to make them run automatically every single day — even on days you don't touch your laptop.

title: Tests That Run While You Sleep
idea:
Your tests currently run "on push" — only when code changes. But Sauce Demo itself could change independently of your code — a deployment on their end could break your selectors with zero commits from you. A scheduled run catches that.

GitHub Actions supports cron schedules — the same scheduling syntax used by servers for decades:

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 6 * * *'

That line means "run at 6:00 AM UTC, every day." Five fields, each one a unit of time: minute, hour, day-of-month, month, day-of-week. An asterisk means "every."
why: I run scheduled test suites for exactly this reason: production environments change even when your test code doesn't. A scheduled run is how you find out a third-party API changed, a certificate expired, or a deployment elsewhere broke something — before a customer finds out for you. This is the difference between automation that reacts to your work and automation that watches the world independently of you.

steps: Add a schedule trigger alongside your existing push trigger, plus workflow_dispatch so you can trigger it manually for testing.
You won't see the scheduled run immediately — GitHub schedules can take up to an hour to trigger for the first time, and won't run at all if your repo's been inactive for 60+ days.
Instead, verify your YAML syntax is valid using GitHub's workflow editor (it flags syntax errors in red before you commit), and manually trigger a run via the Actions tab's "Run workflow" button.

code: on: push: branches: [main] schedule: - cron: '0 6 * * *' workflow_dispatch:
reflection:
"Before today, I thought scheduling automated tasks was [X].
Today, I noticed that writing a cron schedule for my tests made me realize [Y].
That makes me feel [Z] about the difference between automation that reacts to changes and automation that runs independently.
Tomorrow, I will [W]."

further.text: GitHub Actions — Events that trigger workflows (covers schedule, workflow_dispatch, and other triggers)
crontab.guru — paste any cron expression and see it translated into plain English. Use this constantly; nobody memorizes cron syntax.

stretch: Using crontab.guru, write a cron expression for "every weekday at 9 AM UTC" (not weekends). Add it as a second schedule entry. Why might you want weekday-only runs instead of every single day for a test suite tied to a work team's release cadence? Write your reasoning in your reflection.
teaser: Your tests run automatically now. But if one fails at 6 AM and nobody's looking at the Actions tab, did it really get caught? Tomorrow you make failures impossible to miss.
codeRaw:
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 6 * * *'
  workflow_dispatch:

title: Make Failure Impossible to Ignore
idea:
A test that fails silently in a CI log nobody reads might as well not exist. The HTML report Playwright generates is excellent — but only if someone opens it. Today you make sure failure gets noticed without anyone needing to go looking.

GitHub Actions can upload your HTML report as a downloadable artifact automatically, and — more usefully — you can configure your workflow to fail loudly (a red X on your repo, visible from the main GitHub page) the moment any test breaks.

You already have failure visibility through the red X. Today's job is making sure the report itself survives long enough for someone to actually look at it, and that the workflow doesn't quietly swallow a failure.
why: I've seen CI pipelines configured so a failing test still showed a green checkmark — because of a misconfigured continue-on-error flag someone added "temporarily" eight months earlier. The whole point of automation is trustworthy signal. A red X that doesn't mean "something's actually broken" is worse than no automation at all, because it trains people to stop trusting the signal entirely.

steps: Find the actions/upload-artifact step in the Playwright-generated workflow and confirm it has if: always() set — meaning "run this step regardless of whether previous steps passed or failed."
Without if: always(), your report won't upload on a failed run — exactly when you'd want it most.

Push a deliberately broken test (change a selector to something that doesn't exist) to confirm: the Actions tab shows a red X, and the report artifact is still downloadable showing you exactly what failed and why.
Then revert your broken change.

reflection:
"Before today, I thought a failing CI run was [X].
Today, I noticed that confirming if: always() actually worked made me realize [Y] about CI configurations I'll inherit in a real job.
That makes me feel [Z]. Tomorrow, I will [W]."

further.text: GitHub Actions — Storing workflow data as artifacts

stretch: Look up GitHub Actions' built-in notification settings (under your GitHub profile → Settings → Notifications) and confirm you'd be emailed on a failed workflow run. In a real team, this is often routed to Slack instead — search "GitHub Actions Slack notification" to see how teams wire failures into a channel instead of an inbox nobody checks.
teaser: You've built a system that runs itself and reports honestly. Tomorrow you connect every piece from this week into one finished pipeline, end to end.

title: The Full Pipeline
idea:
No new concept today. Today you confirm every piece from this district works together as one coherent system, not five separate exercises:

– Tests organized by feature (Day 22)
– Scoped runs available locally for fast iteration (Day 23)
– Automatic execution on every push (Day 24)
– Scheduled execution independent of code changes (Day 25)
– Honest failure reporting that survives a broken run (Day 26)

This is what "I set up CI for our test suite" actually means when a job description asks for it. Not one of these five things. All five, working together.
why: This is the exact deliverable I'd expect from a TAE three months into their first role. Not flashy. Not the kind of thing that demos well in five minutes. But it's the infrastructure every test suite needs to be trustworthy long after the person who built it has moved to a different project — including, possibly, you moving to a different feature six months from now and needing your past self's system to still make sense.

steps: Write a short README.md at the root of your project covering: what this project is (one sentence), how to run tests locally (the commands from Day 23), folder structure (auth/, inventory/, checkout/, and what each covers), and how CI works (when it runs, where to find results).

Do a final, full run: npx playwright test from a clean pull of your repo (delete node_modules and reinstall if you want to be thorough), confirming everything still passes from scratch — not just on your already-configured machine.

reflection: "Before today, I thought a finished automation project meant [X]. Today, I noticed that writing the README forced me to [Y] about my own project that I hadn't fully thought through before. That makes me feel [Z]. Tomorrow, I will [W]."

further.text: Search "how to write a good README for a code project" — any solid guide on README structure and clarity.

stretch: Add a status badge to the top of your README — GitHub generates these automatically for any workflow, showing a live "passing" or "failing" indicator. Search "GitHub Actions status badge markdown" for the exact syntax. This is a small detail, but it's the kind of polish that signals real engineering discipline in a portfolio project.
